---
title: "MD7115 Part 2"
subtitle: "What Are Graph, Network, and Tree?"
author: '<br><br>Xiaotao Shen <br> Assistant Professor <br> <a href="http://shen-lab.org"> <i class="fa fa-home fa-fw"></i>&nbsp; shen-lab.org</a><br> <a href="mailto:xiaotao.shen@ntu.edu.sg"><i class="fa fa-paper-plane fa-fw"></i>&nbsp; xiaotao.shen@ntu.edu.sg</a><br>'
institute: "Lee Kong Chian School of Medicine, NTU Singapore"
date: '`r Sys.Date()`'
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: [xaringan-themer.css, my-theme.css]
    self_contained: true
    nature:
      ratio: '16:9'
      countdown: false
      titleSlideClass: [center, middle]
      countIncrementalSlides: false
    seal: true
    yolo: false
---

layout: true
background-image: url(images/lkc_logo.png)
background-position: 100% 0%
background-size: 20%

<div class="my-footer"><span>Xiaotao Shen/www.shen-lab.org</span></div>

---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,
  eval = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  cache = FALSE,
  dpi = 72
)
```

# Three related ideas

| Term | Main idea | Typical use |
|---|---|---|
| Graph | Mathematical structure of nodes and edges | Theory and algorithms |
| Network | Graph with real biological meaning | Data analysis and interpretation |
| Tree | Hierarchical special graph | Taxonomy, lineage, evolution |

---

# What is a graph?

.pull-left[
A graph is a set of:

- nodes or vertices
- edges or links

It tells us which objects are connected.
]

.pull-right[
<img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jaspershen-lab/MD7115-NTU/main/3_materials/images/graph.png" alt="Graph" width="70%">
]

---

# What is a network?

A network is a graph with context.

- Nodes represent real biological entities
- Edges represent biologically meaningful relationships
- Attributes add more information to nodes and edges

Examples:

- genes
- proteins
- microbes
- metabolites

---

# What is a tree?

A tree is a graph with a hierarchical structure.

- one connected structure
- no cycles
- parent-child relationships
- useful when ancestry or taxonomy matters

---

# Graph versus network versus tree

- Every tree is a graph
- Many biological networks are graphs
- Not every graph is a tree
- The biological question determines the representation

---

# Biological networks

- Protein-protein interaction network
- Metabolic network
- Correlation network
- Gene regulatory network
- Microbe-metabolite network
- Drug-target network

---

# Protein-protein interaction network

.pull-left[
Nodes:

- proteins

Edges:

- physical or functional interactions
]

.pull-right[
<img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jaspershen-lab/MD7115-NTU/main/3_materials/images/ppi_network.png" alt="PPI network" width="100%">
]

---

# Metabolic network

.pull-left[
Nodes:

- metabolites
- enzymes

Edges:

- reactions
- transformations
]

.pull-right[
<img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jaspershen-lab/MD7115-NTU/main/3_materials/images/metabolic_network.png" alt="Metabolic network" width="100%">
]

---

# Correlation network

.pull-left[
Nodes:

- genes, proteins, metabolites, microbes

Edges:

- pairwise correlations

Important:

- association, not causation
]

.pull-right[
<img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jaspershen-lab/MD7115-NTU/main/3_materials/images/correlation_network.png" alt="Correlation network" width="100%">
]

---

# Tree examples in biology

- phylogenetic tree
- taxonomic tree
- lineage tree
- cell differentiation tree

Use a tree when hierarchy matters more than arbitrary connectivity.

---

# Core components

For any graph or network, define:

1. Node meaning
2. Edge meaning
3. Direction or no direction
4. Weight or no weight
5. Attributes attached to nodes and edges

---

# Common node attributes

- type
- class
- abundance
- p-value
- fold change
- degree
- module assignment

---

# Common edge attributes

- weight
- sign
- confidence
- similarity
- distance
- interaction type

---

# Important network properties

- Degree: how many edges touch a node
- Centrality: how important a node is in the network
- Path: how nodes are connected through the graph
- Community: subgroup structure
- Layout: how the graph is drawn on the page

---

# Degree

.pull-left[
Degree is the number of edges connected to a node.

High-degree nodes are often called hubs.
]

.pull-right[
<img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jaspershen-lab/MD7115-NTU/main/3_materials/images/graph.png" alt="Degree" width="70%">
]

---

# Centrality

Centrality tries to quantify importance.

Examples:

- degree centrality
- betweenness centrality
- closeness centrality

Use the measure that matches your biological question.

---

# Community structure

Communities or modules are groups of nodes that are more connected to each other than to the rest of the network.

Biologically, they may represent:

- pathways
- functional modules
- co-regulated groups

---

# Layout is not biology

Different layouts can make the same graph look very different.

- force-directed layout
- circular layout
- bipartite layout
- tree layout

The layout improves readability. It does not create evidence.

---

# When to use what

- Use a graph when discussing structure or algorithms
- Use a network when relationships have biological meaning
- Use a tree when the data are hierarchical

---

# Transition

Next, we move from concepts to implementation in R.

Main question:

**How do we go from node and edge tables to a useful network figure?**
